common_name: White Nun Orchid (Monja Blanca, Skinner's Lycaste, Guatemala's National Flower)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct light in bark-based medium in a clay pot; Lycaste skinneri is a cool-growing orchid from Guatemala's highlands (4,000–8,000 feet) and requires cool nights (50–60°F) in fall and winter to trigger bloom. Without a cool-dry winter rest, the plant will not flower. Allow the potting medium to dry significantly between waterings during the winter rest (October–February); resume regular watering as new growth starts in spring. The flowers appear on separate stems from the base before or alongside new growth in late winter. Protect from temperatures below 40°F. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct morning light in bark medium in a clay pot — White Nun Orchid grows best in the cool, moist, well-ventilated conditions of highland cloud forest and appreciates the lower temperatures and clay pots that help the roots cool at night. Lycaste skinneri is a deciduous, sympodial epiphytic orchid native to Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador, and Mexico in the Orchidaceae family, bearing the singular distinction of being Guatemala's national flower — honored as the 'Monja Blanca' (White Nun) for the pure white to pale pink of its large, waxy, fragrant flowers and the robe-like arrangement of the broad petals around the smaller, white lip. The plant produces large oval pseudobulbs with broad, pleated leaves that drop in fall before the winter bloom. From the leafless or newly leafing pseudobulbs in late winter to spring, 2–6 individual flowers emerge on separate, erect stems from the base of each pseudobulb — each flower is large (4–6 inches across), waxy, long-lasting (4–6 weeks), with three large, spreading, broadly oval white to pale pink sepals framing two smaller white petals and a small, three-lobed, white, slightly hairy lip. The flowers are lightly fragrant. Wild populations are critically endangered in Guatemala due to habitat loss and collection — all cultivated plants must be from legally propagated nursery stock. Lycaste skinneri has contributed to numerous hybrid orchids. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Lycaste skinneri (Monja Blanca): cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Lycaste skinneri species profile
Guatemala Ministry of Environment — Monja Blanca: national flower conservation
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Lycaste (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Harrison's Bifrenaria (Hairy-Lipped Orchid, Harrison's Orchid, Waxy Bifrenaria)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct morning sun in coarse bark medium or mounted on bark; Bifrenaria harrisoniae is a robust, adaptable epiphyte that tolerates intermediate to warm conditions well. Allow the medium to dry between waterings — this is a relatively drought-tolerant orchid once established. Reduce watering in winter. Blooms in spring on short spikes from the base of the pseudobulbs. Named for Mrs. Arnold Harrison of England, an early collector who first flowered the species in cultivation. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct morning light in coarse, well-draining bark medium — Harrison's Bifrenaria is one of the more tolerant and adaptable orchids for beginners and experienced growers alike, adapting to a range of light and temperature conditions. Bifrenaria harrisoniae is a sympodial, epiphytic to lithophytic orchid native to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most distinctive and immediately recognizable of the Brazilian epiphytic orchids for the combination of its waxy flower texture, color, and hairy lip. The plant produces large, angular, four-ridged pseudobulbs each with a single, large, stiff, leathery, dark-green leaf. In spring, short (4–8 inch) flower spikes emerge from the base of the pseudobulbs bearing 1–4 flowers per spike — each flower is large (2.5–3 inches), waxy, thick, and long-lasting (4–6 weeks), in white to ivory to pale cream with a prominent, three-lobed lip in rich purple-magenta to rose-purple, densely covered in short hairs that give it a softly velvety appearance. The contrast between the clean, waxy, white outer flower and the vivid, hairy, purple lip creates a striking combination unlike most other orchids. The flowers emit a light, pleasant rose-like to violet fragrance. Named in honor of Mrs. Arnold Harrison, an early British orchid enthusiast who first successfully flowered the species from Brazilian collections in the 19th century. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Bifrenaria harrisoniae cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Bifrenaria harrisoniae species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Bifrenaria cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Bifrenaria (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Wormvine Vanilla (Leafless Vanilla, Barefoot Vanilla, Florida Vanilla)

planting.note: Grow on rough-barked trees or cork with exposed aerial roots in partial shade; Vanilla barbellata is a leafless, photosynthetic-root Vanilla species — unlike V. planifolia, it produces no leaves, obtaining all photosynthesis through its flat, green, chlorophyll-containing aerial roots. These roots must be exposed to light (partial shade to filtered light) for the plant to thrive. Wild plants are protected in Florida (endangered) — only cultivate from legally propagated nursery stock. Hand pollinate the short-lived flowers for occasional pod set. Zone 10-12 outdoors. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow on rough-barked trees or cork mounts in filtered, indirect light — Wormvine Vanilla is a leafless climbing orchid that obtains all its photosynthesis through chlorophyll-containing aerial roots that must receive adequate light, making it unlike most orchids in its light requirements for the roots rather than the leaves. Vanilla barbellata is a leafless, climbing, epiphytic orchid native to the Florida Keys, the Bahamas, Cuba, Hispaniola, and Jamaica in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most distinctive of the Vanilla species and a genuinely fascinating botanical curiosity. The plant produces no leaves at any life stage — instead, the flat, strap-like, dark-green aerial roots serve dual function as both anchoring/water-absorbing organs and as photosynthetic structures, containing chloroplasts and performing all the plant's carbon fixation. The stems are thin, wiry, and flexible, climbing by these flatly-adhering roots up tree trunks and branches. The flowers (produced on short racemes from leaf axils) are pale green to white with a white lip, short-lived (single day), and produce occasional small vanilla pods with some fragrance but of much less commercial value than V. planifolia. As a native Florida Keys and Caribbean species, V. barbellata is protected under Florida law and by CITES. In cultivation, it provides a fascinating example of leafless photosynthesis. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Vanilla barbellata: Florida native leafless vanilla
Florida Native Plant Society — Vanilla barbellata species profile
American Orchid Society — Vanilla barbellata: the leafless vanilla vine
Florida Department of Environmental Protection — Protected Native Orchids of Florida

common_name: Yellow Polystachya (Concrete Polystachya, Pan-Tropical Epiphytic Orchid)

planting.note: Grow mounted on rough bark or in a small slatted basket with coarse bark medium in bright indirect to filtered direct morning light; Polystachya concreta is an adaptable, relatively tolerant epiphyte. In zone 9-10, it can be grown outdoors on shaded trees and porches through the warm months and protected indoors in winter. The small, yellow-green flowers with a slightly resupinate lip arrangement (lip on top) are characteristic of the Polystachya genus. Blooms spring–summer. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct light mounted on bark or in a small slatted basket — Yellow Polystachya adapts well to both mounted and potted culture in good indirect light with adequate air circulation. Polystachya concreta is a small, sympodial, epiphytic orchid with one of the widest natural ranges of any orchid species in the world — it occurs naturally across all of tropical Africa, tropical Asia, Australia, the Pacific Islands, the Caribbean, Florida, Mexico, Central America, and tropical South America — a genuinely pantropical distribution that makes it one of the most geographically widespread orchid species known. In Florida, it grows naturally in Highlands, Miami-Dade, Monroe, and Collier Counties as a native epiphyte. The plant produces small, narrow, flattened pseudobulbs with 2–3 narrow, dark-green, somewhat fleshy leaves. In spring and summer, short, erect to arching flower spikes bear 5–20 small (0.5-inch) flowers in yellow-green to yellowish-cream, each with a slightly darker green spotted center and a white to cream three-lobed lip. A distinctive feature of Polystachya flowers is that they are non-resupinate — the lip is held uppermost rather than below as in most orchids, an unusual orientation. While not as showy as many tropical orchids, P. concreta's remarkable adaptability, wide tolerance of conditions, and status as a Florida-native epiphyte make it a meaningful and historically interesting addition to orchid collections. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Polystachya concreta: Florida native and pan-tropical epiphyte
Florida Native Plant Society — Polystachya concreta species profile
American Orchid Society — Polystachya concreta cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Polystachya concreta: global distribution

common_name: Scarlet Ladies' Tresses (Mexican Scarlet Orchid, Cinnabar Orchid, Red Spiranthes)

planting.note: Plant tubers in fall or early spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; Dichromanthus cinnabarinus grows naturally in open woodlands, rocky slopes, and disturbed areas in the highlands of Mexico and Central America (Guatemala, Honduras). It blooms in late summer to fall on erect spikes. Reduce watering in winter dormancy. The brilliant scarlet-red flower spike is one of the most vivid of any spiranthoid orchid. In zone 9, established tubers may overwinter in well-drained beds with mulch. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, moderately lean soil — Scarlet Ladies' Tresses grows most naturally in the well-drained, rocky to sandy soils of open highland woodlands and performs better in lean, well-drained conditions than in rich, moist garden soil. Dichromanthus cinnabarinus is a small, tuberous, deciduous terrestrial orchid in the tribe Cranichideae (subtribe Spiranthinae) native to Mexico and Central America in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most brightly colored of the ladies' tresses-type orchids. Like the white Spiranthes species of the eastern US, Dichromanthus produces a rosette of basal leaves and a spirally-twisted raceme of flowers along an erect spike — but in place of the white or cream of North American Spiranthes, D. cinnabarinus produces flowers in vivid cinnabar-red to brilliant scarlet, one of the most intense flower colors in the entire ladies' tresses group. The individual flowers are tubular, about 0.5 inch long, in bright scarlet to orange-red, arranged in a one- or two-ranked spiral along the erect, 12–24 inch spike. The flowering season is late summer to fall, triggered by the onset of cooler temperatures. Hummingbirds are attracted to the brilliant scarlet flower spikes. A charming and rarely seen spiranthoid orchid for zone 9-11 collectors interested in hardy, ground-dwelling orchids outside the epiphyte mainstream. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Dichromanthus cinnabarinus: Scarlet Ladies' Tresses
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Dichromanthus cinnabarinus species profile
Mexico Comision Nacional para el Conocimiento y Uso de la Biodiversidad — Dichromanthus cinnabarinus
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Spiranthoid orchids of North America and Mexico
